title: Being left alone is not the same as steering your own life
Autonomy and agency are not the same thing
Autonomy is not the same as agency
Autonomy is not agency
Autonomy ≠ agency
define-terms: People often use the words autonomy and agency to mean the same thing. They don't. When life gets hard, you can feel the difference. Autonomy means nobody is telling you what to do. You are free, and no one is getting in your way. Agency is different. Agency means you can pick your own goals, and you can go after them yourself. You can have one of these without the other. Think of a person who is all alone, with nothing they want to do, and no wish to find something to do. That person is free from everyone (autonomous), but they are barely steering their own life (almost no agency).

Autonomy and agency sound like the same thing, but they're not. Autonomy is freedom from interference — nobody is telling you what to do. Agency is your ability to choose your own goals and actually go after them. You can have one without the other. Someone left completely alone, but with no aim and no wish to form one, is autonomous yet has almost no agency.

Autonomy and agency get used as synonyms, but they pull apart under pressure. Autonomy is freedom from interference — nobody is telling you what to do. Agency is the capacity to set your own goals and actually pursue them. You can have one without the other: a person left completely alone but with no aim, and no will to form one, is autonomous and has almost no agency.

Autonomy and agency are treated as synonyms but diverge under pressure. Autonomy is freedom from interference; agency is the capacity to set your own goals and pursue them. They're separable: someone left wholly alone, with no aim and no will to form one, is autonomous yet has almost no agency.
Autonomy ≠ agency. Autonomy: freedom from interference. Agency: capacity to set and pursue one's own goals. Separable — the aimless recluse is fully autonomous, nearly without agency.
ai-drains-agency: AI tends to give you the first one (autonomy) while quietly taking away the second one (agency). It does the boring jobs for you. It answers your question before you have even finished asking it. It takes away the little bumps in the road that used to make you stop and choose for yourself. So your autonomy goes up, because fewer things are asking for your time. But here is the catch. If the AI is also choosing what you read, what you write, and what you should want next, then your agency starts to slip away. You end up more free, but steering your own life less.

AI tends to hand you the first while quietly draining the second. It clears the chores, answers before you've finished asking, and removes the little bumps that used to make you stop and choose. Your autonomy goes up, because fewer things demand your time. But if the AI is also choosing what you read, what you write, and what to want next, your agency slips. You end up freer but steering less.

AI tends to hand you the first while quietly draining the second. It clears the chores, answers before you've finished asking, and removes the friction that used to make you stop and choose. Your autonomy goes up — fewer demands on your time. But if the AI is also choosing what to read, what to write, and what to want next, your agency slips. You're freer and steering less.

AI hands you the first while draining the second. It clears chores, answers before you've finished asking, and removes the friction that once made you stop and choose. Autonomy rises — fewer demands on your time. But when the AI also chooses what you read, write, and want next, agency slips: freer, steering less.
AI grants autonomy, drains agency. Friction removed, choices pre-empted; demands fall while the AI sets what you read, write, and want. Net: freer, steering less.
protect-agency: So look after the second one, agency — not just the first one, freedom. There is a clear way to do this. Keep making the choices that are really yours to make. That is the whole idea behind Delegate the task, keep the judgment. Being free from people getting in your way is worth almost nothing if you have stopped using that freedom to actually do things.

So protect the second, not just the first. The way to do it is concrete: keep making the decisions that are truly yours to make — that's the whole point of Delegate the task, keep the judgment. Freedom from interference is worth little if you've stopped doing anything with it.

So protect the second, not just the first. The defence is concrete: keep deciding the things that are yours to decide, which is the whole point of Delegate the task, keep the judgment. Freedom from interference is worth little if you've stopped doing anything with it.

So protect the second, not just the first. The defence is concrete: keep deciding what's yours to decide — the point of Delegate the task, keep the judgment. Freedom from interference is worthless unused.
Protect agency, not just autonomy. Defence: retain the decisions that are yours — see Delegate the task, keep the judgment. Unused freedom is worthless.
